---
title: "Coordinates — points & grids"
---

Module: `coordinates` (`src/coordinates.f90`). Defines the two geometry types
and their constructors, conversions, I/O, and cell-area calculation.

## Types

### `points_class`

```fortran
type points_class
    character(len=128) :: name        ! dataset name
    character(len=128) :: mtype       ! latlon | cartesian | stereographic | ...
    character(len=128) :: units       ! degrees | kilometers | meters | ...
    type(planet_class) :: planet      ! WGS84 or Spherical Earth

    logical :: is_cartesian, is_projection
    logical :: is_lon180              ! .TRUE. => lon in [-180,180]; .FALSE. => [0,360]
    type(projection_class) :: proj

    integer :: npts
    real(dp), allocatable :: x(:), y(:), lon(:), lat(:), area(:)
    real(dp), allocatable :: dx(:), dy(:)   ! per-point cell spacing (for conservative)
    integer,  allocatable :: border(:)
    real(dp) :: xy_conv               ! axis-units -> meters conversion factor
end type
```

### `grid_class`

Same descriptive fields as `points_class`, but the location arrays are 2D and a
structured-axis descriptor `G` is attached:

```fortran
type grid_class
    ! ... same metadata fields as points_class ...
    integer :: npts
    real(dp), allocatable :: x(:,:), y(:,:), lon(:,:), lat(:,:), area(:,:)
    integer,  allocatable :: border(:,:)
    real(dp) :: xy_conv
    type(grid_axis_class) :: G
end type

type grid_axis_class
    integer  :: nx, ny
    real(dp) :: x0, dx, y0, dy
    real(dp), allocatable :: x(:), y(:)
end type
```

## Building a grid — `grid_init`

`grid_init` is a generic interface with four constructors. The most common is
**from options**:

```fortran
subroutine grid_init_from_opts(grid, name, mtype, units, &
                               planet, lon180, &
                               x, y, x0, dx, nx, y0, dy, ny, &
                               lambda, phi, alpha, x_e, y_n)
```

| Argument | Required | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `grid` | ✔ (out) | the grid object to fill |
| `name` | ✔ | grid name (used in cached map filenames) |
| `mtype` | ✔ | coordinate/map type string |
| `units` | ✔ | axis units |
| `planet` | – | `"WGS84"` (default) or `"Spherical Earth"` |
| `lon180` | – | longitude convention |
| `x, y` | – | explicit axis vectors (alternative to `x0/dx/nx`) |
| `x0, dx, nx` | – | x-axis origin / spacing / count |
| `y0, dy, ny` | – | y-axis origin / spacing / count |
| `lambda, phi, alpha` | – | projection centre lon, centre lat, oblique angle |
| `x_e, y_n` | – | false easting / northing |

Other constructors in the interface:

| Constructor | Builds a grid from… |
|---|---|
| `grid_init_from_opts` | explicit options (above) |
| `grid_init_from_par` | a parameter file (`filename`) |
| `grid_init_from_grid` | another grid (copy + override axes) |
| `grid_init_from_points` | an existing `points_class` |

### Examples

```fortran
! Global 2° lat-lon grid
call grid_init(g, name="CCSM3-T42", mtype="latlon", units="degrees", &
               x0=0.d0, dx=2.d0, nx=180, y0=-90.d0, dy=2.d0, ny=90)

! Regional oblique-stereographic grid over Greenland
call grid_init(g, name="GRL-20KM", mtype="stereographic", units="kilometers", &
               dx=20.d0, nx=76, dy=20.d0, ny=151, &
               lambda=-40.d0, phi=72.d0, alpha=7.5d0)
```

## Building a point set — `points_init`

Generic interface with five constructors. From options:

```fortran
subroutine points_init_from_opts(pts, name, mtype, units, &
                                 planet, lon180, &
                                 x, y, dx, dy, &
                                 lambda, phi, alpha, x_e, y_n, &
                                 latlon, verbose)
```

`x(:)` and `y(:)` are the required location vectors. If `latlon=.TRUE.` the
supplied `x,y` are interpreted as `lon,lat`. Per-point `dx,dy` may be supplied
for conservative remapping. The other constructors mirror `grid_init`:

| Constructor | Builds points from… |
|---|---|
| `points_init_from_opts` | explicit `x,y` vectors + options |
| `points_init_from_par` | a parameter file |
| `points_init_from_file` | a data file (with `skip` header lines) |
| `points_init_from_points` | another point set |
| `points_init_from_grid` | a grid |

## Converting between representations

```fortran
subroutine grid_to_points(grid, pts, mask_pack, define)   ! 2D grid -> 1D points
subroutine points_to_grid(pts, grid, mask_pack, define)   ! 1D points -> 2D grid
```

`mask_pack(:,:)` (optional, logical) restricts the conversion to selected cells
— this is how the library works with sparse subsets of a grid. `define`
controls whether the target object's metadata is (re)initialised.

## Allocation & comparison helpers

```fortran
call grid_allocate(grid, var)      ! allocate var to grid shape; generic over
                                   ! integer / float / double / logical
call points_allocate(pts, var)     ! same, for a point set

logical = compare_coord(pts1, pts2)   ! do two sets share a coordinate system?
logical = compare_coord(grid1, grid2)
```

`grid_allocate` / `points_allocate` are the idiomatic way to size a field
array to match a grid or point set without restating dimensions.

## Nearest point

```fortran
idx = pts_which_nearest(pts, x, y, latlon)   ! index of nearest point to (x,y)
```

## Cell areas

Cell area is computed when a grid is initialised (`grid_area`,
`coordinates.f90:141`). For each cell the four corner vertices are constructed
(extending by the cell spacing at boundaries, midpoints in the interior) and
the area is integrated:

- **Cartesian** grids → shoelace formula in the working units
  (`cartesian_area`).
- **Projected** grids → each corner is inverse-projected to lon/lat, then the
  spherical/ellipsoidal polygon area is integrated (`planet_area`, using
  GeographicLib).
- **Lat-lon** grids → `planet_area` directly on the lon/lat corners.

Areas are divided by `xy_conv²` so they end up in the grid's own units. For a
bare `points_class` (no grid structure) area defaults to `1.0` until set by a
`grid_to_points` conversion.

## I/O & printing

```fortran
call grid_write(grid, filename, ...)     ! write grid coordinates to NetCDF
call points_write(pts, filename, ...)
call grid_print(grid)                     ! summary to stdout
call points_print(pts)
```
